ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): From a methodological-engineer perspective the paper offers notable novelty by formulating a fine-grained product-identification task in an under-studied domain (cybercrime marketplaces) and by assembling and releasing a sizeable annotated dataset across four forums — a concrete resource and problem framing that are new to the community. The modeling approaches themselves largely reuse established techniques (latent SVM, Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume-style feature duplication), so the algorithmic contribution is incremental; however the empirical focus on cross-forum domain-adaptation failure modes and the analyses separating inventory vs. stylistic transfer provide insights that would inform experts and are clearly differentiated from prior work. | B(3): The paper provides a useful, domain-specific dataset and frames a fine-grained cross-forum domain-adaptation task for identifying products in cybercrime marketplaces—an application-level contribution that is not widely explored and thus adds novel problem framing and empirical analysis (93,924 posts, 1,938 annotated). However, the modeling techniques are largely standard (latent SVM, Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III feature duplication) and do not introduce new algorithms; the main novelty lies in the dataset, evaluation protocol (token/NP/post-level) and empirical insights about cross-forum degradation and vocabulary vs. stylistic causes. From my perspective as a domain-impact reviewer this is a solid, moderate-novelty contribution rather than a highly original methodological advance.

reviewer_a.justification: From a methodological-engineer perspective the paper offers notable novelty by formulating a fine-grained product-identification task in an under-studied domain (cybercrime marketplaces) and by assembling and releasing a sizeable annotated dataset across four forums — a concrete resource and problem framing that are new to the community. The modeling approaches themselves largely reuse established techniques (latent SVM, Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume-style feature duplication), so the algorithmic contribution is incremental; however the empirical focus on cross-forum domain-adaptation failure modes and the analyses separating inventory vs. stylistic transfer provide insights that would inform experts and are clearly differentiated from prior work.

However, the modeling techniques are largely standard (latent SVM, Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III feature duplication) and do not introduce new algorithms; the main novelty lies in the dataset, evaluation protocol (token/NP/post-level) and empirical insights about cross-forum degradation and vocabulary vs. stylistic causes. From my perspective as a domain-impact reviewer this is a solid, moderate-novelty contribution rather than a highly original methodological advance.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses an information-extraction task with a clear domain-adaptation focus, releases a sizeable forum dataset (93,924 posts with 1,938 annotations), and targets problems (cross-forum generalization) of broad interest. The formulation (token- and NP-level annotations, projection to noun phrases), the use of latent SVMs with standard domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III), and evaluation metrics (token/NP F1, post accuracy, Fleiss’ kappa) meet community standards. From a methodological-reviewer perspective the experimental design isolates within- vs cross-forum performance and probes OOV/type effects, making it highly relevant to the venue’s audience and expectations. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it introduces a novel, realistic dataset (93,924 posts with 1,938 annotated) and a clearly formulated fine-grained domain-adaptation task grounded in cybercrime marketplaces, which is of clear interest to both NLP and security communities. The methodology and evaluation use standard learning-based systems, appropriate baselines (gazetteers, Brown clusters, Daume III), and sensible metrics (token/NP F1, post accuracy, Fleiss' kappa), and the analyses of in-vocabulary vs out-of-vocabulary performance and cross-forum transfer provide actionable insight for researchers. From a domain-impact perspective the work addresses an important real-world problem and situates itself correctly within prior domain-adaptation and information-extraction literature, making it highly relevant to the venue.

The formulation (token- and NP-level annotations, projection to noun phrases), the use of latent SVMs with standard domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III), and evaluation metrics (token/NP F1, post accuracy, Fleiss’ kappa) meet community standards. From a methodological-reviewer perspective the experimental design isolates within- vs cross-forum performance and probes OOV/type effects, making it highly relevant to the venue’s audience and expectations.

The methodology and evaluation use standard learning-based systems, appropriate baselines (gazetteers, Brown clusters, Daume III), and sensible metrics (token/NP F1, post accuracy, Fleiss' kappa), and the analyses of in-vocabulary vs out-of-vocabulary performance and cross-forum transfer provide actionable insight for researchers. From a domain-impact perspective the work addresses an important real-world problem and situates itself correctly within prior domain-adaptation and information-extraction literature, making it highly relevant to the venue.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): This paper makes a useful, practical contribution by assembling and releasing a reasonably large, annotated dataset (93,924 posts, 1,938 annotated) for a neglected but important extraction problem (products in cybercrime forums) and by empirically documenting large cross-forum degradation and the limited efficacy of standard semi-supervised/domain-adaptation methods (Brown clusters, gazetteers, feature duplication). From a methodological-engineering perspective those findings and the fine-grained analyses (in-vocabulary vs out-of-vocabulary, inventory vs stylistic effects) will steer future work and provide a clear experimental benchmark, but the contribution is more enabling than transformative — it motivates follow-up methods rather than immediately changing prevailing approaches or yielding broadly generalizable solutions. | B(4): This paper addresses an important and underexplored practical problem—extracting product mentions from online cybercrime marketplaces—and contributes a sizable multi-forum dataset (93,924 posts with 1,938 annotated) that fills a clear gap for practitioners and researchers studying illicit marketplaces. Its empirical finding that supervised models suffer large cross-forum degradation and that standard semi-supervised/domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, feature duplication) give limited gains is likely to change how researchers approach domain robustness for security-related IE tasks. By separating in-vocabulary vs. out-of-vocabulary performance and analyzing inventory and stylistic causes of transfer failure, the work opens substantive new research directions (better cross-domain representations, annotation strategies, and evaluation protocols) that are likely to be built upon by both NLP and security communities. The contribution therefore has broad implications beyond the immediate subfield by highlighting real-world domain-shift challenges in applied IE.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper makes a useful, practical contribution by assembling and releasing a reasonably large, annotated dataset (93,924 posts, 1,938 annotated) for a neglected but important extraction problem (products in cybercrime forums) and by empirically documenting large cross-forum degradation and the limited efficacy of standard semi-supervised/domain-adaptation methods (Brown clusters, gazetteers, feature duplication). From a methodological-engineering perspective those findings and the fine-grained analyses (in-vocabulary vs out-of-vocabulary, inventory vs stylistic effects) will steer future work and provide a clear experimental benchmark, but the contribution is more enabling than transformative — it motivates follow-up methods rather than immediately changing prevailing approaches or yielding broadly generalizable solutions.

By separating in-vocabulary vs. out-of-vocabulary performance and analyzing inventory and stylistic causes of transfer failure, the work opens substantive new research directions (better cross-domain representations, annotation strategies, and evaluation protocols) that are likely to be built upon by both NLP and security communities. The contribution therefore has broad implications beyond the immediate subfield by highlighting real-world domain-shift challenges in applied IE.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (dataset release, task formulation, cross-forum evaluation). Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and the use of tables/figures and concrete examples (token- vs NP-level evaluation, per-forum analyses) supports the narrative. Writing quality is generally good with adequate related-work coverage, though a few methodological descriptions (e.g., the NP projection and some domain-adaptation experimental details) could be clearer or expanded for reproducibility. From a methodological-reviewer perspective the presentation is solid but would benefit from slightly more explicit exposition in those sections. | B(4): From the provided summary the paper is well organized (task framing, dataset construction, methods, experiments, analyses, conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early. Notation and metrics appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and the writing quality is described as good; figures/tables and example analyses are present and informative. Minor presentation issues may remain in places (e.g., token- vs. NP-level evaluation mechanics could require clearer exposition for readers unfamiliar with projection), but overall the manuscript is well-written and accessible to the intended audience.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (dataset release, task formulation, cross-forum evaluation). Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and the use of tables/figures and concrete examples (token- vs NP-level evaluation, per-forum analyses) supports the narrative. Writing quality is generally good with adequate related-work coverage, though a few methodological descriptions (e.g., the NP projection and some domain-adaptation experimental details) could be clearer or expanded for reproducibility. From a methodological-reviewer perspective the presentation is solid but would benefit from slightly more explicit exposition in those sections.

reviewer_b.justification: From the provided summary the paper is well organized (task framing, dataset construction, methods, experiments, analyses, conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early. Notation and metrics appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and the writing quality is described as good; figures/tables and example analyses are present and informative. Minor presentation issues may remain in places (e.g., token- vs. NP-level evaluation mechanics could require clearer exposition for readers unfamiliar with projection), but overall the manuscript is well-written and accessible to the intended audience.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are strong: multi-forum dataset (93,924 posts, 1,938 annotated), evaluation at token/NP/post levels, and analyses separating in-vocab vs out-of-vocab product performance that substantiate the main cross-domain degradation claim. Appropriate ablations for modeling choices are present (+Brown, +Gazetteer, Daume III) and limitations/negative results (limited gains from common domain-adaptation techniques, annotation cost) are explicitly discussed. However, important gaps remain for full reproducibility and methodological robustness: code release isn’t mentioned, the annotated set is small relative to unlabeled data, and the paper explores only a narrow set of semi-supervised/domain-adaptation methods without reported statistical significance or broader sensitivity analyses. These make the study solid but not exhaustive. | B(4): The paper presents a substantial empirical study across four real-world forums with both token- and NP-level evaluation, domain adaptation baselines (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III), and analyses (in-vocab vs OOV, inventory vs style effects) that convincingly support the main claims. Ablation-style comparisons among feature additions are present, and hyperparameters are reported, but more diverse adaptation methods and clearer release of code/data would strengthen reproducibility and breadth. Related work appears adequately covered and the paper candidly discusses negative results and cross-forum limitations. Overall, the experiments are solid and well-targeted to the claims, with only minor gaps in extensiveness and release details.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are strong: multi-forum dataset (93,924 posts, 1,938 annotated), evaluation at token/NP/post levels, and analyses separating in-vocab vs out-of-vocab product performance that substantiate the main cross-domain degradation claim. Appropriate ablations for modeling choices are present (+Brown, +Gazetteer, Daume III) and limitations/negative results (limited gains from common domain-adaptation techniques, annotation cost) are explicitly discussed. However, important gaps remain for full reproducibility and methodological robustness: code release isn’t mentioned, the annotated set is small relative to unlabeled data, and the paper explores only a narrow set of semi-supervised/domain-adaptation methods without reported statistical significance or broader sensitivity analyses. These make the study solid but not exhaustive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a substantial empirical study across four real-world forums with both token- and NP-level evaluation, domain adaptation baselines (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III), and analyses (in-vocab vs OOV, inventory vs style effects) that convincingly support the main claims. Ablation-style comparisons among feature additions are present, and hyperparameters are reported, but more diverse adaptation methods and clearer release of code/data would strengthen reproducibility and breadth. Related work appears adequately covered and the paper candidly discusses negative results and cross-forum limitations. Overall, the experiments are solid and well-targeted to the claims, with only minor gaps in extensiveness and release details.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by empirical evidence: a new four-forum dataset with token-level annotations, cross-forum experiments showing large transfer drops, and analyses (in-/out-of-vocab, inventory vs. style) that isolate failure modes. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to vet. The experimental methodology is broadly sound with reasonable baselines (dictionary, post-level, Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III), phrase-level projection, and reported hyperparameters, but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and reproducibility details (code release is not stated), which weakens confidence in some comparative claims. Overall the results justify the conclusions, but minor gaps in statistical analysis and reproducibility prevent a top score. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments: it assembles a sizable multi-forum dataset (93,924 posts, 1,938 annotated), shows large cross-forum degradation, and evaluates reasonable baselines (dict, post-level, Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III). Experimental methodology appears sound with appropriate baselines and hyperparameter reporting, though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing and the NP-projection via an automatic parser may introduce unquantified noise—minor technical gaps. Overall the conclusions about limited gains from common semi-supervised and domain-adaptation techniques are justified by the presented analyses, but the missing discussion/measurement of parser-induced errors and statistical tests prevents a flawless technical endorsement.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by empirical evidence: a new four-forum dataset with token-level annotations, cross-forum experiments showing large transfer drops, and analyses (in-/out-of-vocab, inventory vs. style) that isolate failure modes. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to vet. The experimental methodology is broadly sound with reasonable baselines (dictionary, post-level, Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III), phrase-level projection, and reported hyperparameters, but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and reproducibility details (code release is not stated), which weakens confidence in some comparative claims. Overall the results justify the conclusions, but minor gaps in statistical analysis and reproducibility prevent a top score.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a sensible set of baselines (dictionary, post-level model) and common domain-adaptation or semi-supervised techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III), evaluated on the same datasets and metrics and with hyperparameters reported. The authors honestly report when prior techniques give limited or inconsistent gains and provide analyses (in-vocabulary vs out-of-vocabulary, inventory vs stylistic shifts) that help isolate failure modes. Minor omissions prevent a top score: the summary does not indicate comparison to more recent neural sequence-labeling or modern domain-adaptation methods (or release of code), so the evaluation is thorough but not fully comprehensive against the latest alternatives. | B(5): The paper evaluates a broad set of appropriate baselines for this fine-grained domain adaptation IE task (dictionary baseline, post-level model, Brown clusters, gazetteers, and Daumé III feature duplication), using the same datasets and metrics across within- and cross-forum setups. It honestly reports limited gains from standard semi-supervised and domain-adaptation methods and provides analyses (in-vocabulary vs out-of-vocabulary, inventory vs stylistic differences) that clearly dissect where baselines succeed or fail. Related work coverage is noted as adequate, and the comparison framing appears fair and comprehensive for the claims made.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a sensible set of baselines (dictionary, post-level model) and common domain-adaptation or semi-supervised techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, Daume III), evaluated on the same datasets and metrics and with hyperparameters reported. main_claims: Formulates and studies the task of identifying products in online cybercrime marketplaces with a fine-grained domain adaptation focus.
Assembles and releases a dataset covering four forums (Darkode, Hack Forums, Blackhat, Nulled) comprising 93,924 posts with 1,938 posts annotated for product mentions.
Shows that supervised models suffer large performance degradation in cross-forum (cross-domain) settings and that common semisupervised and domain-adaptation techniques provide limited gains.
Provides analyses separating in-vocabulary vs. out-of-vocabulary product performance and demonstrates that product inventory differences and stylistic conventions both affect cross-domain transfer.
_summary.methodology: Token-level annotation of product mentions anchored to posts, projection to noun-phrases via an automatic parser for phrase-level evaluation, and learning-based systems (latent SVM trained with subgradient/AdaGrad) augmented with features such as Brown clusters, gazetteers (type-level supervision), and domain-feature duplication (Daume III) to study within- and cross-forum performance.
datasets: Released dataset: 93,924 posts with 1,938 annotated posts
limitations: Performance is severely degraded in cross-forum settings compared to within-forum evaluation.
Standard semisupervised and domain-adaptation techniques (Brown clusters, gazetteers, feature duplication) give limited and inconsistent improvements.
Token-level annotation is time-consuming, and type-level gazetteers only partially mitigate unseen-product issues.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - information extraction and domain adaptation for cybercrime marketplace forums

reviewer_a.name: Methodological Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP extraction systems in industry and academia, experienced in experimental design, reproducible pipelines, and productionizing models.
traits: Carefully checks experimental design choices: train/dev/test splits, cross-validation, and whether results reflect proper controls.
Demands clear baselines and ablation studies to justify each modeling choice and reported gain.
Values reproducibility: expects hyperparameter details, training curves, statistical significance tests, and code/data release instructions.
Focuses on evaluation protocol fidelity: label projection methods, matcher thresholds, and error analysis that isolates failure modes.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical, evidence-driven reviewer who prioritizes methodological clarity, thorough ablations, and reproducibility so empirical claims are well supported.

reviewer_b.name: Domain Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Principal investigator and researcher at the intersection of NLP and cybersecurity, with a track record of building datasets and assessing their scientific and societal impact.
traits: Assesses novelty and contribution relative to prior datasets and domain-adaptation work in both NLP and cybercrime analysis.
Examines annotation schema and dataset coverage for real-world relevance, including class balance, forum selection, and annotation quality.
Evaluates broader impact: utility for downstream tasks, potential for misuse, ethical considerations, and how well analyses illuminate actionable insights for the security community.
Values clear framing of limitations and concrete guidance for future work and adoption by practitioners.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Big-picture, domain-aware reviewer who weighs novelty, dataset value, and real-world impact while expecting the paper to situate itself clearly within prior work and ethical constraints.
